title: Promises & async/await
summary: Learn why JavaScript needs async code, how Promises track work in progress, and how async/await makes the whole thing read like a story.

blocks.md: Imagine ordering a coffee. You don't stand frozen at the counter until it's ready — you step aside, the barista makes it, and they call your name when it's done. That's exactly how JavaScript handles slow work: instead of grinding to a halt waiting for a network request or a timer, it starts the task, moves on, and comes back when the result is ready. This pattern is called **asynchronous programming**, and mastering it unlocks the most powerful, real-world parts of JavaScript.

JavaScript runs on a **single thread** — picture one chef in a kitchen. If that chef stops everything to wait for the oven timer, nobody gets any food. Browsers work the same way: if your code blocks the thread (say, by waiting for a server response), the entire page freezes — buttons stop responding, animations stall, and users leave.

The solution: hand slow tasks off to the browser's built-in helpers (network, timers, etc.) and only handle the result when it arrives, without ever stopping the main thread.

blocks.title: The Ticket Stub Analogy
blocks.md: A **Promise** is like a ticket stub at a coat check. The moment you hand over your coat, you get a stub — a *placeholder* for the coat you'll pick up later. The coat isn't in your hands yet, but you have proof the work is in progress. When you come back, either the coat is ready (**fulfilled**) or it was lost (**rejected**). The stub is what you pass around in the meantime.

blocks.text: The Three States of a Promise

blocks.md:
Every Promise lives in exactly one of three states:
- **Pending** — the async work is still in progress.
- **Fulfilled** — the work completed successfully and produced a value.
- **Rejected** — something went wrong and produced an error.

Once a Promise moves from *pending* to either state, it never changes again.

blocks.caption: new Promise takes an executor function. resolve() fulfills it; reject() rejects it. .then handles success; .catch handles failure.

blocks.md:
`.then` chains work, but they can get messy. **async/await** is syntactic sugar on top of Promises — the same mechanism, but written like normal top-to-bottom code. Two rules:
1. Mark a function with `async` to allow `await` inside it.
2. `await` pauses *only that function* until the Promise settles — the rest of the app keeps running.

blocks.caption: await unwraps the resolved value of a Promise. Much easier to read than chained .then calls.
A real-world fetch example: await the response, then await the JSON. Errors from either step are caught in one place.

blocks.title: await only works inside an async function
Run independent tasks in parallel with Promise.all
blocks.md:
Awaiting Promises one by one runs them in *series*. For independent tasks, use `Promise.all` to run them in parallel — much faster:
```js
const [users, posts] = await Promise.all([fetchUsers(), fetchPosts()]);
```

blocks.question: What does `await` do when placed in front of a Promise?
options: It blocks the entire JavaScript engine until the Promise resolves.
It pauses only the current async function until the Promise settles, then resumes it with the resolved value.
It converts the Promise into a synchronous value immediately.
It cancels the Promise if it takes too long.
blocks.explanation: await pauses the async function it lives inside, freeing the event loop to do other work. Once the Promise settles, the function resumes right where it left off. The rest of the program keeps running normally — nothing is blocked.

keyTakeaways: JavaScript is single-threaded, so async patterns let slow tasks run without freezing the page.
A Promise has three states: pending, fulfilled, and rejected — and it never goes back.
Use .then/.catch to chain Promise handling, or async/await for cleaner, readable code.
Always wrap awaited code in try/catch to handle rejections gracefully.
Use Promise.all to run independent async tasks in parallel instead of one at a time.

exercise.prompt:
Write an async function called `getUserName` that:
1. Uses `fetch` to GET `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1`
2. Parses the JSON response
3. Returns the user's `name` property
4. Catches any errors and logs them with `console.error`

Then call the function and log the returned name.
exercise.hint: Remember: fetch() returns a Promise, so await it. Then call .json() on the response — that is also a Promise, so await that too. The user object has a `name` field you can return directly.
